calendar.matters:
Amended Notice Of Verified Petition For Writ Of Mandate
Off Calendar Nov-30-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Corey A. Evans, Esq. of Evans & Page, Tel. 415.637.2354, for petitioner and moving party, by Court Call;
No other appearance.

RULING: Petitioner's counsel stated his objections to the court's tentative ruling. At the conclusion of the hearing, the Court adopted its tentative ruling. PETITIONER KATHLEEN MURPHY'S Petition For Writ Of Mandate is GRANTED in part. The Statement of Decision dated April 10, 2017 issued by Hearing Officer J. Denny is vacated and the issue of whether Ms. Murphy's dog Charlie is a vicious and dangerous dog may be reconsidered by Mr. Denny in accordance with this order. Mr. Denny may choose to either: 1) hold a new hearing limited to evidence and argument about the February 13, 2013 incident and, based on both the April 6, 2017 hearing and the new hearing, issue a new statement of decision or 2) issue a new statement of decision solely based on the April 6, 2017 hearing without consideration of or reference to the February 13, 2013 incident. Mr. Denny's consideration of and reliance on evidence and argument about the February 13, 2013 incident at the April 6, 2017 hearing and in his April 10, 2017 statement of decision without giving Ms. Murphy explicit notice in advance of the April 6 hearing that he would or might do so violated Ms. Murphy's common law right of fair procedure to be informed in advance of the hearing of the scope of the issues under consideration at the hearing. Mr. Denny is not barred by either the statute of limitations or the doctrine of laches from considering the February 13, 2013 incident in determining whether Charlie is a vicious and dangerous dog. There is nothing unfair or improper about consideration of the February 13, 2013 incident to determine in 2017 or perhaps 2018 whether Charlie is a vicious and dangerous dog, there has been no showing that such consideration is legally prejudicial to Ms. Murphy, and arguably public safety supports consideration of that incident. The statutory and case law cited by Ms. Murphy on the application of statute of limitations and laches is inapposite. Because the March 13, 2013 incident must be either considered anew after another hearing or be deleted from any consideration if no new hearing is held, it is not strictly necessary to address Ms. Murphy's argument that Mr. Denny abused his discretion by concluding that a bite occurs anytime a dog's tooth makes contact with a human and there is blood. Nonetheless, for guidance in the event that this issue may arise again, the court is of the view that a hearing officer has wide discretion, within the constraints imposed by the applicable sections of the San Francisco Health Code, to determine what conduct does or does not support a determination that a dog is a vicious and dangerous dog. Until January 31, 2018 or such earlier date that Mr. Denny may issue a new statement of decision or decline to do so, Ms. Murphy must comply with all the requirements of the Health Code applicable to a vicious and dangerous dog with respect to Charlie.

ORDER SIGNED AFTER HEARING. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Denise Doucette, CSR#5963, Tel. 707.246.3323.
